meta.title: 2.3 Browser-Based Execution (WebContainers)
meta.description: Learn how WebContainers runs Node.js inside the browser via WebAssembly — intercepting POSIX system calls, emulating a filesystem, routing network through a ServiceWorker — and what native module incompatibility means for Make's package support decisions.

blocks.prompt:
StackBlitz claims to run Node.js entirely in your browser tab. How is that possible? Node.js needs a filesystem, network access, and child processes — none of which exist in a browser. Write down your best hypothesis before reading.

---

blocks.content:
## What You'll Learn

This module explains how WebContainers fakes a full Node.js environment inside a browser tab using WebAssembly and a ServiceWorker, why this eliminates server compute costs entirely, and where the capability ceiling sits. By the end, you'll be able to explain which parts of a generated full-stack app can run in a WebContainer and which parts cannot — and what that means for Make's preview infrastructure decisions.

WebContainers moves the execution cost from your servers to the user's browser, enabling near-zero-cost instant previews — but native modules, real database connections, and raw TCP access are architectural impossibilities in this model, which places a hard ceiling on the complexity of apps that can be previewed without a server-side component.

**The core insight:** Node.js itself is just software. Software can be compiled to WebAssembly (WASM — a binary format that browsers can execute at near-native speed; any language that compiles to machine code can also compile to WASM). WebAssembly runs in browsers. Therefore, in principle, Node.js can run in a browser. The trick is providing everything Node.js expects to find when it starts up.

**What WebContainers actually does:**

Node.js assumes it's running on an operating system. It expects to call `open()` to open files, `read()` to read them, `write()` to write them, `socket()` to create network connections. These are POSIX system calls (the standard operating system API that Unix-like systems expose to programs) that don't exist in a browser tab.

WebContainers intercepts these calls at the boundary between the Node.js WASM binary and the browser. When Node.js tries to read `/package.json`, WebContainers serves it from an in-memory virtual filesystem (a JavaScript object tree that maps paths to file contents). When Node.js opens a TCP socket on port 3000, WebContainers intercepts that and routes it through a ServiceWorker (a browser background script that can intercept and respond to network requests) that rewrites requests destined for `localhost:3000` to be served from the in-memory server.

The result is that Node.js — running as WASM in a browser tab — genuinely believes it has a real filesystem and real network stack. It doesn't. Everything is emulated in JavaScript and WebAssembly.

**What works:**

- `npm install` — the npm CLI runs in the WASM Node.js, resolves the dependency tree, downloads packages from the real npm registry (through the browser's actual network), and writes them to the virtual filesystem
- Vite dev server — it boots, watches files in the virtual filesystem, and serves via the ServiceWorker
- Most pure-JavaScript packages — anything that only uses Node.js standard APIs that WebContainers emulates
- React, Vue, Svelte, SolidJS, Astro — all work because their build tools are pure JS
- TypeScript compilation (transpilation — the process of converting TypeScript to JavaScript that browsers can run) — same reason

---

blocks.question: WebContainers emulates a filesystem and network stack inside the browser. When `npm install` runs and downloads packages from the real npm registry, which part of that process is real and which part is emulated?
blocks.answer: The network download is real — the browser makes genuine HTTP requests to the npm CDN over the actual internet. What is emulated is where the downloaded packages go: instead of writing to a real filesystem on disk, WebContainers writes them to an in-memory virtual filesystem (a JavaScript object tree). The npm CLI running as WASM believes it is writing to `/node_modules/`, but those writes are intercepted and stored as JavaScript objects in browser memory. This is why an IndexedDB cache is needed for persistence — without it, closing the tab destroys the virtual filesystem and all installed packages.

blocks.content:
**What doesn't work:**

- **Native modules** — packages like `bcrypt`, `sharp`, `canvas`, `sqlite3`, or database drivers that ship precompiled C++ binaries (`.node` files) cannot be loaded because there's no mechanism to run arbitrary native code in the browser sandbox
- **Direct TCP/UDP** — you can fake HTTP, but you cannot open raw sockets; real database connections (PostgreSQL, MongoDB, Redis) are impossible because they use custom wire protocols over TCP (the low-level internet protocol that moves raw data — databases speak custom protocols on top of it, not HTTP)
- **Heavy CPU workloads** — the WASM Node.js runs slower than native Node.js; CPU-intensive work (video transcoding, large matrix operations) will be noticeably slow and may exhaust browser memory
- **Child process spawning** — `child_process.exec()` and `spawn()` don't work; you can't shell out to system commands
- **Long-running background processes** — the browser tab is the process boundary; if the tab closes, everything stops

**Why this matters for Make's preview infrastructure:**

The zero-cost-per-user property is transformative. A container-based preview costs ~$0.01-0.05 per active hour per user in compute costs. A WebContainer preview costs $0 in compute (the user's browser does the work). At 100,000 monthly active users each spending 2 hours in preview, that's the difference between $2,000-10,000/month in infrastructure and $0.

The startup time advantage is equally significant. A WebContainer environment is ready in under a second from cold. The filesystem is in-memory, the npm packages are cached in the browser's IndexedDB (a browser-based database that persists across page loads — WebContainers uses it to avoid re-downloading packages on every session), and the Vite dev server starts in ~200ms. HMR (Hot Module Replacement — the mechanism that updates a running app in the browser without a full page reload when code changes) happens in milliseconds because everything is in the same memory space.

**Bolt (by StackBlitz) uses this model.** This is not speculation — Bolt's architecture is built on WebContainers. When you prompt Bolt to generate an app, the code runs in your browser tab. This means Bolt's previews are instant and its compute costs for previews are near-zero. It's a structurally different cost model than competitors using server-side containers.

## How to Think About This

The WebContainers model is essentially a cost-shifting trade: the platform pays zero compute, but the user's browser does the work. The browser becomes both the IDE and the server. What you gain is instant startup and zero infrastructure cost for the preview layer. What you lose is the ability to run anything that requires OS-level access — native binaries, raw TCP sockets, persistent processes. The capability ceiling is not a bug that can be engineered away; it is inherent to the browser security model. Every product decision about WebContainers eventually runs into this ceiling, and knowing where it sits is what separates informed tradeoff discussions from surprises late in development.

**The real constraints for Make:**

The core limitation is that any feature requiring native packages, real database connections, or server-side computation cannot live in the WebContainer layer. If a user's generated app needs to talk to a PostgreSQL database, that database and the code connecting to it must live server-side. The WebContainer can render the frontend; it cannot run the backend in those cases.

Browser tab memory limits (~2-4GB on modern Chrome) also create a ceiling. A project with thousands of files and large `node_modules` can approach this limit.

---

blocks.question: A user prompts Make: "Build me a full-stack todo app with a PostgreSQL database." Your preview infrastructure is WebContainers. Which parts of this app can run in the WebContainer, and which parts cannot? What does your infrastructure need to provide for the parts that can't?
blocks.answer:
The React frontend, the API route code (as JavaScript), and the Vite/Next.js dev server can run in the WebContainer. The PostgreSQL database cannot — it's a native binary with a TCP wire protocol. The connection from the app to the database also can't be a real TCP connection. Options: (1) use an in-browser SQLite via WebAssembly (e.g., sql.js) as a proxy for PostgreSQL during preview, (2) provision a real Postgres instance server-side and proxy requests through the ServiceWorker, or (3) mock the database layer entirely for preview purposes. Each is a product tradeoff between fidelity and cost.*

---

blocks.title:
Go to [stackblitz.com](https://stackblitz.com) and create a new Vite + React project. Open the browser DevTools → Network tab. Watch the requests as `npm install` runs. Notice: packages are being fetched from the real npm registry, but the server serving your app at localhost is... your own browser tab. Open a second tab to the same URL — notice it doesn't work (the ServiceWorker is per-tab).

Then open the Application tab → IndexedDB — you'll see the cached filesystem. This is the persistence layer.

---
blocks.solution: During `npm install`, the Network tab should show HTTP requests going to registry.npmjs.org or a CDN like cdn.jsdelivr.net — these are real network requests to download package tarballs. But the server responding to your app's requests on localhost:port is not a remote server; it's the ServiceWorker running in your own tab. You can confirm this by looking at the Network tab entries for your app's assets — they'll show as 'ServiceWorker' in the initiator column. When you open a second tab to the same StackBlitz URL, it won't load correctly because the ServiceWorker that intercepts localhost requests is scoped to the original tab — there's no shared server to connect to. In the Application → IndexedDB panel, you should see a database entry with a path structure that mirrors a filesystem — this is how StackBlitz persists your virtual filesystem across browser sessions so you don't lose your work when you close the tab. If you don't see these things clearly, try hard-refreshing and starting a fresh project; the WebContainers boot sequence is most visible on the first load.

blocks.prompt:
A journalist is writing about Bolt versus a traditional cloud IDE. Without using "WebAssembly" or "ServiceWorker," explain why Bolt can offer free unlimited previews while a traditional cloud IDE has to charge for compute time.

---

questions: Think of a product decision you made — or observed — where the cost model of an underlying technology turned out to be a hidden constraint on what the product could offer for free. How was the cost discovered, and how did it change the product's direction?

When you've evaluated a new technical approach for a product feature, have you ever encountered a hard capability ceiling — something the approach fundamentally cannot do — that only became visible late in the process? What would have surfaced it earlier?

---

blocks.content:
WebContainers moves the execution cost from your servers to the user's browser, enabling near-zero-cost instant previews — but native modules, real database connections, and raw TCP access are architectural impossibilities in this model, which places a hard ceiling on the complexity of apps that can be previewed without a server-side component.

---

blocks.prompt:
WebContainers handles the browser execution case. But generated apps need to run somewhere after the user leaves the preview tab — and many apps need server-side logic even during preview. What are the lightweight server-side execution options between "full Docker container" and "nothing"?

---

---

reviewQuestions.question: When Node.js runs inside WebContainers and tries to read a file, what actually happens?
options: Node.js reads from the browser's localStorage, which WebContainers maps to a flat file path structure
Node.js's file system call is intercepted and served from an in-memory virtual filesystem — a JavaScript object tree mapping file paths to contents
Node.js tunnels the file read to a remote server via a background WebSocket connection
reviewQuestions.explanation: WebContainers intercepts POSIX system calls at the boundary between the Node.js WASM binary and the browser. File operations are served from an in-memory virtual filesystem — a JavaScript object tree that maps paths to file contents. Node.js genuinely believes it has a real filesystem. Nothing touches localStorage or a remote server.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Well done — the in-memory virtual filesystem is one of the two core mechanisms that makes Node.js believe it's running on a real OS.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: WebContainers uses an in-memory virtual filesystem — a JavaScript object tree — not localStorage. File operations are intercepted by WebContainers before they reach the browser's own storage APIs. Node.js never knows the filesystem is emulated.

reviewQuestions.question: Which of the following npm packages would fail to install or run correctly in a WebContainers preview?
options: react-router-dom, because it uses browser history APIs that WebContainers cannot emulate
axios, because it opens raw TCP connections that ServiceWorkers cannot intercept
bcrypt, because it ships a precompiled C++ binary (.node file) that cannot execute in a browser sandbox
reviewQuestions.explanation: bcrypt ships a precompiled native C++ binary (.node file). WebContainers runs Node.js as WebAssembly in the browser, which cannot load native binaries compiled for a specific OS. This is the hard ceiling the module identifies: no native modules, no raw TCP, no child processes. react-router-dom and axios are pure JavaScript and work fine in WebContainers.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Right — native modules like bcrypt that ship compiled C++ binaries are a hard architectural impossibility in WebContainers, not a configuration problem.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: react-router-dom is pure JavaScript and works fine in WebContainers. axios uses the Fetch API, which WebContainers supports. bcrypt is the problem: it ships a precompiled C++ binary, and WebContainers cannot execute native binaries in the browser sandbox — that's an architectural impossibility, not a configuration issue.

reviewQuestions.question: Approximately how much does it cost Make per active hour per user to run preview sessions using WebContainers versus a container-based approach?
options: WebContainers costs near-zero for Make's servers because execution runs in the user's browser; containers cost approximately $0.01–0.05 per active hour per user
WebContainers costs roughly the same as containers — around $0.01–0.05 per hour — because CDN bandwidth replaces compute cost
WebContainers costs more than containers because serving the WebAssembly Node.js binary requires high-bandwidth CDN delivery
reviewQuestions.explanation: The module provides the specific comparison: container-based preview runs at $0.01–0.05 per active hour in server compute. WebContainers transfers execution to the user's browser, making the server-side compute cost near-zero. At 100,000 monthly active users spending 2 hours in preview, that's $2,000–10,000/month versus near-zero.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Nice — that cost shift is the structural economic advantage of the WebContainers model, and understanding those specific numbers makes it a real product argument.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The WebAssembly delivery cost is minimal compared to active compute. WebContainers' near-zero server cost comes from shifting execution to the user's browser entirely. Containers charge $0.01–0.05 per active hour per user — at 100,000 users spending 2 hours each, that's $2,000–10,000/month vs. near-zero for WebContainers.
